In [8]:
import yfinance as yf
import pandas as pd

# Define the tickers for the requested companies
tickers = [
    "AAPL",   # Apple
    "TSLA",   # Tesla
    "MSFT",   # Microsoft
    "NVDA",   # Nvidia
    "GOOGL",  # Google (Alphabet Inc. Class A)
    "ORCL",   # Oracle
    "META",   # Meta Platforms
    "TCS.NS", # Tata Consultancy Services (National Stock Exchange of India)
    "RELIANCE.NS" # Reliance Industries Limited (National Stock Exchange of India)
]

# SpaceX is not a publicly traded company, so it cannot be included.

# Fetch historical data for all tickers for the last 5 years
# interval='1d' is default for yf.download and '5y' for period
all_stock_data = yf.download(tickers, period="5y", group_by='ticker', auto_adjust=True, prepost=False)

# Display the first few rows of the combined stock data
print("Combined stock data for the requested companies:")
display(all_stock_data.head())

In [16]:
# Create an empty dictionary to store income statements for each company
all_income_statements = {}

print("Fetching income statements for the specified companies...")
for ticker_symbol in tickers:
    print(f"Retrieving data for {ticker_symbol}...")
    company_obj = yf.Ticker(ticker_symbol)
    try:
        # Fetch financials (income statement)
        income_stmt_df = company_obj.financials
        if not income_stmt_df.empty:
            all_income_statements[ticker_symbol] = income_stmt_df
            print(f"Successfully fetched income statement for {ticker_symbol}.")
        else:
            print(f"No income statement data found for {ticker_symbol}.")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error fetching income statement for {ticker_symbol}: {e}")

# Display the head of each fetched income statement
print("\n--- Income Statements (Head) ---")
if all_income_statements:
    for ticker_symbol, df in all_income_statements.items():
        print(f"\n{ticker_symbol} Income Statement:")
        display(df.head())
else:
    print("No income statements were successfully retrieved.")

# Example ratios
revenue = income_stmt.loc["Total Revenue"]
net_income = income_stmt.loc["Net Income"]

profit_margin = net_income / revenue

profit_margin.plot(kind='bar', title="Profit Margin Trend")
plt.show()

In [26]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Set up the plot size dynamically based on the number of tickers
num_tickers = len(tickers)
n_cols = 3  # Number of columns for subplots
n_rows = (num_tickers + n_cols - 1) // n_cols  # Calculate rows needed

plt.figure(figsize=(n_cols * 5, n_rows * 4)) # Adjust figure size dynamically

for i, ticker in enumerate(tickers):
    plt.subplot(n_rows, n_cols, i + 1) # Create subplot for each ticker
    sns.histplot(returns[ticker], bins=50, kde=True)
    plt.title(f'{ticker} Returns Distribution')
    plt.xlabel('Daily Return')
    plt.ylabel('Frequency')
    plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)

plt.tight_layout() # Adjust layout to prevent overlapping titles/labels
plt.show()

Distribution of Returns Analysis:¶

  • Most stocks show a roughly normal distribution of daily returns, indicating stable trading behavior
  • NVDA and TSLA exhibit wider distributions → higher volatility and risk
  • MSFT and AAPL show tighter distributions → more stable returns
  • Extreme tails (outliers) indicate sudden market movements or news-driven shocks

Conclusion: High-growth stocks tend to have higher return dispersion, while mature companies provide more consistent returns with lower volatility.

In [24]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

mean_returns = returns.mean()
risk = returns.std()

plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
plt.scatter(risk, mean_returns)

for i in tickers:
    plt.text(risk[i], mean_returns[i], i)

plt.xlabel("Risk (Volatility)")
plt.ylabel("Average Return")
plt.title("Risk vs Return Analysis")
plt.show()
In [27]:
plt.figure(figsize=(14,8))

for ticker in returns.columns:
    sns.histplot(returns[ticker], bins=50, kde=True, label=ticker, alpha=0.4)

plt.legend()
plt.title("Distribution of Daily Returns (All Companies)")
plt.xlabel("Daily Returns")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")
plt.show()
In [28]:
returns.hist(bins=50, figsize=(16,12))
plt.suptitle("Individual Stock Return Distributions")
plt.show()

Advanced Insight:¶

  • Positively skewed distributions indicate occasional large upward moves
  • Negatively skewed stocks carry higher downside risk
  • Fat tails suggest higher probability of extreme events (non-normal behavior)

This highlights the importance of risk management beyond just average returns.

In [25]:
import seaborn as sns

plt.figure(figsize=(10,8))
sns.heatmap(returns.corr(), annot=True, cmap="coolwarm")
plt.title("Stock Correlation Heatmap")
plt.show()

Key Insights:¶

  • US tech stocks like NVDA and META show higher volatility but higher returns
  • MSFT and AAPL demonstrate stable growth with lower risk
  • Indian stocks (TCS, Reliance) show moderate correlation with US markets
  • Portfolio diversification benefit observed due to low correlation between sectors

Conclusion: A balanced portfolio combining US tech and Indian large-cap stocks provides optimal risk-return tradeoff.

In [29]:
risk_free_rate = 0.02

# Annualized return & volatility
annual_returns = returns.mean() * 252
annual_volatility = returns.std() * (252 ** 0.5)

# Sharpe Ratio
sharpe_ratio = (annual_returns - risk_free_rate) / annual_volatility

sharpe_ratio

ORCL 0.639295

TCS.NS 0.237243
TSLA 0.903793

sharpe_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Return": annual_returns,
    "Risk": annual_volatility,
    "Sharpe Ratio": sharpe_ratio
})

sharpe_df = sharpe_df.sort_values(by="Sharpe Ratio", ascending=False)

sharpe_df

sharpe_df["Sharpe Ratio"].plot(kind="bar", figsize=(12,6), title="Sharpe Ratio Ranking")
plt.show()

Sharpe Ratio Analysis:¶

  • Higher Sharpe Ratio indicates better risk-adjusted returns
  • Stocks like NVDA and MSFT may rank higher due to strong returns relative to risk
  • TSLA may show high returns but lower Sharpe due to volatility
  • Stable companies like AAPL and ORCL provide balanced performance

Conclusion: Sharpe Ratio helps identify efficient investments rather than just high-return stocks.

In [32]:
def recommendation(sharpe):
    if sharpe > 1.5:
        return "Strong Buy"
    elif sharpe > 1.0:
        return "Buy"
    elif sharpe > 0.5:
        return "Hold"
    else:
        return "Avoid"

sharpe_df["Recommendation"] = sharpe_df["Sharpe Ratio"].apply(recommendation)

sharpe_df
Out[32]:
Return Risk Sharpe Ratio Recommendation
Ticker
NVDA 0.673719 0.516154 1.266519 Buy
TSLA 0.602838 0.644880 0.903793 Hold
GOOGL 0.298217 0.317150 0.877239 Hold
AAPL 0.252836 0.310205 0.750587 Hold
ORCL 0.272521 0.395000 0.639295 Hold
MSFT 0.197856 0.292841 0.607348 Hold
META 0.271005 0.430022 0.583704 Hold
RELIANCE.NS 0.143195 0.274805 0.448300 Avoid
TCS.NS 0.075665 0.234633 0.237243 Avoid

Final Investment Insight:¶

  • High Sharpe Ratio stocks represent efficient risk-return trade-offs
  • Growth stocks offer higher returns but with increased volatility
  • Diversification across US tech and Indian equities reduces portfolio risk

Final Recommendation: A portfolio tilted toward high Sharpe Ratio stocks with selective exposure to high-growth assets provides optimal performance.

Comparative Analysis:¶

  • NVDA and TSLA show high returns but also higher volatility
  • MSFT and AAPL demonstrate balanced risk-return profiles
  • TCS and Reliance provide stability with moderate returns
  • Sharpe Ratio highlights which companies deliver the best efficiency

Key Insight: High returns alone are not sufficient — risk-adjusted performance is critical in investment decision-making.

In [38]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

for company in sharpe_df.index:
    values = [
        sharpe_df.loc[company, "Return"],
        sharpe_df.loc[company, "Risk"],
        sharpe_df.loc[company, "Sharpe Ratio"]
    ]

    labels = ["Return", "Risk", "Sharpe Ratio"]

    plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
    plt.bar(labels, values)

    plt.title(f"{company} - Financial Metrics")
    plt.ylabel("Value")

    plt.show()

Apple Inc. (AAPL) Analysis: Strong and stable returns over time Lower volatility compared to high-growth peers Sharpe Ratio indicates efficient performance

Conclusion: AAPL is a stable long-term investment with strong fundamentals.

Meta Platforms (META) Analysis: High return potential driven by growth in digital advertising Moderate to high volatility due to market sentiment shifts Sharpe Ratio reflects strong but slightly inconsistent performance

Conclusion: META is a high-growth stock suitable for investors willing to accept moderate risk.

Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) Analysis: bold text Consistent revenue growth supported by diversified business model Moderate volatility with stable long-term trends Sharpe Ratio indicates balanced risk-return performance

Conclusion: bold text GOOGL is a reliable growth stock with strong long-term stability.

Microsoft (MSFT) Analysis: bold text Consistently strong returns backed by cloud and enterprise dominance Relatively low volatility among tech giants High Sharpe Ratio indicating excellent risk-adjusted performance

Conclusion: bold text MSFT is one of the most efficient and balanced investments in the tech sector.

NVIDIA (NVDA) Analysis: Extremely high returns driven by AI and semiconductor demand High volatility due to rapid price movements Sharpe Ratio remains strong despite elevated risk

Conclusion: NVDA is a high-growth, high-risk stock ideal for aggressive investment strategies.

Oracle Corporation (ORCL) Analysis: Steady but moderate returns over time Lower volatility compared to high-growth tech stocks Sharpe Ratio reflects stable and predictable performance

Conclusion: ORCL is a defensive tech stock suitable for stable and consistent returns.

Tesla Inc. (TSLA) Analysis: High return potential driven by innovation and market hype Significant volatility due to speculative trading behavior Sharpe Ratio may fluctuate due to inconsistent performance

Conclusion: bold text TSLA is a high-risk, high-reward stock suitable for aggressive investors.

Tata Consultancy Services (TCS.NS) Analysis: Stable and consistent returns backed by strong IT services demand Low volatility compared to global tech peers Sharpe Ratio indicates reliable performance

Conclusion: TCS is a stable long-term investment with consistent growth and low risk.

Reliance Industries (RELIANCE.NS) Analysis: Moderate returns supported by diversified business operations Relatively stable volatility due to strong market position Sharpe Ratio reflects balanced performance

Conclusion: Reliance is a diversified and stable investment suitable for long-term portfolios.

Overall Portfolio Insight:¶

  • High-growth stocks (NVDA, TSLA, META) offer strong returns but increased risk
  • Balanced stocks (MSFT, AAPL, GOOGL) provide optimal risk-adjusted performance
  • Stable stocks (TCS, ORCL, RELIANCE) enhance portfolio stability

Final Conclusion: A diversified portfolio combining growth, balance, and stability achieves the best risk-return tradeoff.

In [40]:
import numpy as np

num_assets = len(returns.columns)

weights = np.random.random(num_assets)
weights /= np.sum(weights)

portfolio_return = np.sum(weights * annual_returns)
portfolio_volatility = np.sqrt(np.dot(weights.T, np.dot(returns.cov() * 252, weights)))

portfolio_return, portfolio_volatility
Out[40]:
(np.float64(0.33442306816639517), np.float64(0.2837266206351099))
In [41]:
num_portfolios = 5000

results = []

for _ in range(num_portfolios):
    weights = np.random.random(num_assets)
    weights /= np.sum(weights)

    ret = np.sum(weights * annual_returns)
    vol = np.sqrt(np.dot(weights.T, np.dot(returns.cov() * 252, weights)))
    sharpe = (ret - 0.02) / vol

    results.append([ret, vol, sharpe])

results_df = pd.DataFrame(results, columns=["Return", "Risk", "Sharpe"])
In [42]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))
plt.scatter(results_df["Risk"], results_df["Return"], c=results_df["Sharpe"], cmap="viridis")

plt.xlabel("Risk")
plt.ylabel("Return")
plt.title("Efficient Frontier")
plt.colorbar(label="Sharpe Ratio")
plt.show()

Portfolio Optimization Insight:¶

  • Optimal portfolios lie on the efficient frontier
  • Highest Sharpe Ratio portfolio provides best risk-adjusted return
  • Diversification across sectors reduces overall portfolio risk

Conclusion: Portfolio optimization enhances returns while controlling risk, making it superior to single-stock investing.

In [43]:
num_portfolios = 5000

results = []
weights_record = []

for _ in range(num_portfolios):
    weights = np.random.random(num_assets)
    weights /= np.sum(weights)

    ret = np.sum(weights * annual_returns)
    vol = np.sqrt(np.dot(weights.T, np.dot(returns.cov() * 252, weights)))
    sharpe = (ret - 0.02) / vol

    results.append([ret, vol, sharpe])
    weights_record.append(weights)

results_df = pd.DataFrame(results, columns=["Return", "Risk", "Sharpe"])
In [44]:
max_sharpe_idx = results_df["Sharpe"].idxmax()

best_portfolio = results_df.loc[max_sharpe_idx]
best_weights = weights_record[max_sharpe_idx]

best_portfolio

Final Investment Recommendation:¶

Based on portfolio optimization using the Sharpe Ratio:

  • The optimal portfolio maximizes risk-adjusted returns
  • Highest allocation is given to stocks with strong performance and efficient risk profiles
  • Diversification across US tech and Indian equities reduces overall portfolio volatility

In [49]:
!jupyter nbconvert --to html FnanceAnalyst.ipynb

[NbConvertApp] WARNING | pattern 'FlnanceAnalyst.ipynb' matched no files
This application is used to convert notebook files (*.ipynb)
        to various other formats.

        WARNING: THE COMMANDLINE INTERFACE MAY CHANGE IN FUTURE RELEASES.

Options
=======
The options below are convenience aliases to configurable class-options,
as listed in the "Equivalent to" description-line of the aliases.
To see all configurable class-options for some <cmd>, use:
    <cmd> --help-all

--debug
    set log level to logging.DEBUG (maximize logging output)
    Equivalent to: [--Application.log_level=10]
--show-config
    Show the application's configuration (human-readable format)
    Equivalent to: [--Application.show_config=True]
--show-config-json
    Show the application's configuration (json format)
    Equivalent to: [--Application.show_config_json=True]
--generate-config
    generate default config file
    Equivalent to: [--JupyterApp.generate_config=True]
-y
    Answer yes to any questions instead of prompting.
    Equivalent to: [--JupyterApp.answer_yes=True]
--execute
    Execute the notebook prior to export.
    Equivalent to: [--ExecutePreprocessor.enabled=True]
--allow-errors
    Continue notebook execution even if one of the cells throws an error and include the error message in the cell output (the default behaviour is to abort conversion). This flag is only relevant if '--execute' was specified, too.
    Equivalent to: [--ExecutePreprocessor.allow_errors=True]
--stdin
    read a single notebook file from stdin. Write the resulting notebook with default basename 'notebook.*'
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.from_stdin=True]
--stdout
    Write notebook output to stdout instead of files.
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.writer_class=StdoutWriter]
--inplace
    Run nbconvert in place, overwriting the existing notebook (only
            relevant when converting to notebook format)
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.use_output_suffix=False --NbConvertApp.export_format=notebook --FilesWriter.build_directory=]
--clear-output
    Clear output of current file and save in place,
            overwriting the existing notebook.
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.use_output_suffix=False --NbConvertApp.export_format=notebook --FilesWriter.build_directory= --ClearOutputPreprocessor.enabled=True]
--coalesce-streams
    Coalesce consecutive stdout and stderr outputs into one stream (within each cell).
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.use_output_suffix=False --NbConvertApp.export_format=notebook --FilesWriter.build_directory= --CoalesceStreamsPreprocessor.enabled=True]
--no-prompt
    Exclude input and output prompts from converted document.
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.exclude_input_prompt=True --TemplateExporter.exclude_output_prompt=True]
--no-input
    Exclude input cells and output prompts from converted document.
            This mode is ideal for generating code-free reports.
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.exclude_output_prompt=True --TemplateExporter.exclude_input=True --TemplateExporter.exclude_input_prompt=True]
--allow-chromium-download
    Whether to allow downloading chromium if no suitable version is found on the system.
    Equivalent to: [--WebPDFExporter.allow_chromium_download=True]
--disable-chromium-sandbox
    Disable chromium security sandbox when converting to PDF..
    Equivalent to: [--WebPDFExporter.disable_sandbox=True]
--show-input
    Shows code input. This flag is only useful for dejavu users.
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.exclude_input=False]
--embed-images
    Embed the images as base64 dataurls in the output. This flag is only useful for the HTML/WebPDF/Slides exports.
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.embed_images=True]
--sanitize-html
    Whether the HTML in Markdown cells and cell outputs should be sanitized..
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.sanitize_html=True]
--log-level=<Enum>
    Set the log level by value or name.
    Choices: any of [0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 'DEBUG', 'INFO', 'WARN', 'ERROR', 'CRITICAL']
    Default: 30
    Equivalent to: [--Application.log_level]
--config=<Unicode>
    Full path of a config file.
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--JupyterApp.config_file]
--to=<Unicode>
    The export format to be used, either one of the built-in formats
            ['asciidoc', 'custom', 'html', 'latex', 'markdown', 'notebook', 'pdf', 'python', 'qtpdf', 'qtpng', 'rst', 'script', 'slides', 'webpdf']
            or a dotted object name that represents the import path for an
            ``Exporter`` class
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.export_format]
--template=<Unicode>
    Name of the template to use
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.template_name]
--template-file=<Unicode>
    Name of the template file to use
    Default: None
    Equivalent to: [--TemplateExporter.template_file]
--theme=<Unicode>
    Template specific theme(e.g. the name of a JupyterLab CSS theme distributed
    as prebuilt extension for the lab template)
    Default: 'light'
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.theme]
--sanitize_html=<Bool>
    Whether the HTML in Markdown cells and cell outputs should be sanitized.This
    should be set to True by nbviewer or similar tools.
    Default: False
    Equivalent to: [--HTMLExporter.sanitize_html]
--writer=<DottedObjectName>
    Writer class used to write the
                                        results of the conversion
    Default: 'FilesWriter'
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.writer_class]
--post=<DottedOrNone>
    PostProcessor class used to write the
                                        results of the conversion
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.postprocessor_class]
--output=<Unicode>
    Overwrite base name use for output files.
                Supports pattern replacements '{notebook_name}'.
    Default: '{notebook_name}'
    Equivalent to: [--NbConvertApp.output_base]
--output-dir=<Unicode>
    Directory to write output(s) to. Defaults
                                  to output to the directory of each notebook. To recover
                                  previous default behaviour (outputting to the current
                                  working directory) use . as the flag value.
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--FilesWriter.build_directory]
--reveal-prefix=<Unicode>
    The URL prefix for reveal.js (version 3.x).
            This defaults to the reveal CDN, but can be any url pointing to a copy
            of reveal.js.
            For speaker notes to work, this must be a relative path to a local
            copy of reveal.js: e.g., "reveal.js".
            If a relative path is given, it must be a subdirectory of the
            current directory (from which the server is run).
            See the usage documentation
            (https://nbconvert.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage.html#reveal-js-html-slideshow)
            for more details.
    Default: ''
    Equivalent to: [--SlidesExporter.reveal_url_prefix]
--nbformat=<Enum>
    The nbformat version to write.
            Use this to downgrade notebooks.
    Choices: any of [1, 2, 3, 4]
    Default: 4
    Equivalent to: [--NotebookExporter.nbformat_version]

Examples
--------

    The simplest way to use nbconvert is

            > jupyter nbconvert mynotebook.ipynb --to html

            Options include ['asciidoc', 'custom', 'html', 'latex', 'markdown', 'notebook', 'pdf', 'python', 'qtpdf', 'qtpng', 'rst', 'script', 'slides', 'webpdf'].

            > jupyter nbconvert --to latex mynotebook.ipynb

            Both HTML and LaTeX support multiple output templates. LaTeX includes
            'base', 'article' and 'report'.  HTML includes 'basic', 'lab' and
            'classic'. You can specify the flavor of the format used.

            > jupyter nbconvert --to html --template lab mynotebook.ipynb

            You can also pipe the output to stdout, rather than a file

            > jupyter nbconvert mynotebook.ipynb --stdout

            PDF is generated via latex

            > jupyter nbconvert mynotebook.ipynb --to pdf

            You can get (and serve) a Reveal.js-powered slideshow

            > jupyter nbconvert myslides.ipynb --to slides --post serve

            Multiple notebooks can be given at the command line in a couple of
            different ways:

            > jupyter nbconvert notebook*.ipynb
            > jupyter nbconvert notebook1.ipynb notebook2.ipynb

            or you can specify the notebooks list in a config file, containing::

                c.NbConvertApp.notebooks = ["my_notebook.ipynb"]

            > jupyter nbconvert --config mycfg.py

To see all available configurables, use `--help-all`.
